title: What Do We Mean by Intelligence?
question: Is intelligence one thing, many things, or a label placed over a bundle of capacities?
why_i_followed_this: The book often treats intelligence as a single scale that can keep rising. I kept wondering whether that framing hides important differences between reasoning, memory, planning, adaptation, creativity, and social understanding.
learning_objectives: Distinguish intelligence from cognition, competence, reasoning, and generality.
Compare psychometric, computational, ecological, and embodied accounts of intelligence.
Evaluate whether a single scalar measure can represent broad cognitive capability.
Identify how benchmark design shapes what researchers call intelligent behavior.
seminar_questions: What is lost when intelligence is treated as a single dimension?
Can a system be broadly capable without possessing anything like human general intelligence?
How much of intelligence depends on tools, environments, and social systems?
What evidence would justify calling an artificial system generally intelligent?

methods_lab.title: Operationalizing Intelligence
methods_lab.prompt: Design three operational definitions of intelligence for the same artificial system: one psychometric, one ecological, and one task-performance based.
methods_lab.deliverable: A construct table with measures, validity threats, and likely failure cases.

position_memo.prompt: Should intelligence be modeled as a scalar, a profile, a relational property, or something else?

my_current_position: I do not think intelligence is best understood as one clean quantity. I currently see it as a profile of capacities whose importance changes across environments, tasks, and social contexts.
what_would_change_my_mind: A theory that consistently predicts transfer, adaptation, and failure across very different domains using a genuinely unified measure would move me closer to a scalar account.
connected_trails: How Do We Know an AI System Is Safe?

core_readings.title: On the Measure of Intelligence
core_readings.creator: François Chollet
core_readings.note: Argues skill at specific tasks is a poor proxy for intelligence and proposes measuring generalization/sample-efficiency directly — a clean empirical anchor for the scalar-vs-profile question.

core_readings.title: Universal Intelligence: A Definition of Machine Intelligence
core_readings.creator: Shane Legg & Marcus Hutter
core_readings.note: The most-cited attempt at a single formal, computable definition of intelligence — useful as the strongest 'scalar' position to argue against or refine.

core_readings.title: Frames of Mind: The Theory of Multiple Intelligences
core_readings.creator: Howard Gardner
core_readings.note: The classic critique of a single general-intelligence view. The theory itself is contested in psychometrics, which is useful to flag in the construct table.

core_readings.title: Intelligence Without Representation
core_readings.creator: Rodney Brooks
core_readings.note: Foundational embodied/ecological account: intelligence as situated behavior rather than internal symbolic reasoning.

further_reading.title: The Measure of All Minds: Evaluating Natural and Artificial Intelligence
further_reading.creator: José Hernández-Orallo
further_reading.note: Full psychometric-computational synthesis — the most systematic treatment of construct validity for intelligence measures.

further_reading.title: Why AI Is Harder Than We Think
further_reading.creator: Melanie Mitchell
further_reading.note: Directly addresses how benchmark design creates illusions of generality — pairs well with the benchmark-artifact seminar question.

further_reading.title: Beyond IQ: A Triarchic Theory of Human Intelligence
further_reading.creator: Robert Sternberg
further_reading.note: EXCERPT ONLY - Analytic, creative, and practical intelligence as separable components — useful as a human-cognition profile model to compare against AI capability profiles.

further_reading.title: A Collection of Definitions of Intelligence
further_reading.creator: Shane Legg & Marcus Hutter
further_reading.note: Survey of ~70 published definitions of intelligence from psychology, AI, and biology — raw material for the methods-lab construct table.

title: Optimization, Goals, and Agency
question: When does optimization become agency?
why_i_followed_this: I kept returning to whether increasingly persistent optimization becomes genuine agency, or whether I was collapsing two different things. The book often moves quickly from capability to goal pursuit, and I wanted a more principled boundary.
learning_objectives: Distinguish optimization, competence, planning, goal-directedness, and agency.
Compare behavioral, mechanistic, and philosophical accounts of agency.
Evaluate how memory, tools, feedback loops, and human scaffolding affect the system boundary.
Identify assumptions behind claims about persistent goals and power-seeking.
seminar_questions: Is agency a property of a model, a deployed system, or an observer's explanatory stance?
Can a system pursue goals without representing them explicitly?
Does increasing persistence create agency gradually, or is there a principled boundary?
When humans provide memory, tools, and task decomposition, who or what is the agent?

methods_lab.title: Operationalizing Agency
methods_lab.prompt: Develop three measurable definitions of agency and test each against the same hypothetical AI system.
methods_lab.deliverable: A one-page construct table with definitions, measures, failure cases, and limitations.

position_memo.prompt: Is agency binary, continuous, multidimensional, or observer-relative?

my_current_position: Agency should be separated from intelligence, cognition, and sentience. I currently understand it as multidimensional and partly dependent on the larger system in which the model is embedded.
what_would_change_my_mind: Evidence of stable, cross-context goal representations that causally organize long-horizon behavior without external scaffolding would move me toward a stronger model-intrinsic account.
connected_trails: What Do We Mean by Intelligence?

core_readings.title: Discovering Agents
core_readings.creator: Zachary Kenton, Ramana Kumar, Sebastian Farquhar, Jonathan Richens, Matt MacDermott & Tom Everitt
core_readings.note: The first formal causal definition of agency in ML systems — an agent is a system that would change its policy if its actions influenced the world differently. Directly usable in the construct table.

core_readings.title: The Intentional Stance
core_readings.creator: Daniel Dennett
core_readings.note: Argues agency/intentionality can be an observer's explanatory stance rather than an intrinsic property — the philosophical anchor for the observer-relative seminar question.

core_readings.title: Optimal Policies Tend to Seek Power
core_readings.creator: Alexander Matt Turner, Logan Smith, Rohin Shah, Andrew Critch & Prasad Tadepalli
core_readings.note: Formal result showing power-seeking emerges from broad reward-optimization under mild assumptions — the technical backbone for 'when does optimization become agency.'

further_reading.title: Risks from Learned Optimization in Advanced Machine Learning Systems
further_reading.creator: Evan Hubinger, Chris van Merwijk, Vladimir Mikulik, Joar Skalse & Scott Garrabrant
further_reading.note: Introduces mesa-optimization: a learned model can become an optimizer with its own objective, distinct from the outer training objective.

further_reading.title: The Basic AI Drives
further_reading.creator: Stephen Omohundro
further_reading.note: Early argument for instrumental convergence — that almost any goal creates incentives toward self-preservation and resource acquisition, prefiguring the power-seeking literature.

further_reading.title: Intention, Plans, and Practical Reason
further_reading.creator: Michael Bratman
further_reading.note: Philosophy-of-action account of planning agency in humans — a useful ground truth for comparing against 'planning' in RL/LLM agent systems.

further_reading.title: Power-Seeking Can Be Probable and Predictive for Trained Agents
further_reading.creator: Victoria Krakovna & Janos Kramar
further_reading.note: Extends the Turner et al. results from optimal to trained (RL-learned) policies — closes the gap between theory and realistic agents.

title: Minds Made of Machines
question: What would justify treating an artificial system as a mind rather than merely a mechanism?
why_i_followed_this: Questions about sentience, consciousness, subjectivity, and moral status kept sitting underneath the technical claims. I wanted to separate what a system can do from whether there is anything it is like to be that system.
learning_objectives: Distinguish consciousness, sentience, cognition, intelligence, subjectivity, and moral status.
Compare functionalist, biological, computational, and embodied theories of mind.
Evaluate proposed indicators of machine consciousness and their limitations.
Analyze how uncertainty about consciousness should affect ethical treatment.
seminar_questions: Is functional equivalence enough for consciousness?
What evidence could distinguish genuine experience from convincing simulation?
Should moral precaution apply before machine consciousness can be demonstrated?
Does embodiment matter for subjectivity?

methods_lab.title: Indicators Without Certainty
methods_lab.prompt: Construct an evidence matrix for machine consciousness using behavioral, architectural, functional, developmental, and self-report evidence.
methods_lab.deliverable: A two-page evidence matrix with confidence levels and unresolved ambiguities.

position_memo.prompt: What evidentiary threshold should be required before an artificial system receives moral consideration?

my_current_position: I think intelligence, agency, and consciousness must remain separate questions. I am open to machine consciousness, but current behavioral fluency is not enough to establish it.
what_would_change_my_mind: Converging evidence from architecture, persistent self-modeling, cross-context reports, causal interventions, and theory-driven indicators would move me toward treating a system as a plausible moral patient.
connected_trails: What Do We Mean by Intelligence?

core_readings.title: Consciousness in Artificial Intelligence: Insights from the Science of Consciousness
core_readings.creator: Patrick Butlin, Robert Long, et al.
core_readings.note: The most systematic attempt to derive testable 'indicator properties' of consciousness from neuroscience theories and apply them to current AI. Concludes no current systems qualify, but no obvious technical barrier either.

core_readings.title: What Is It Like to Be a Bat?
core_readings.creator: Thomas Nagel
core_readings.note: The foundational statement of the subjective-character-of-experience problem — why functional description may leave something out.

core_readings.title: Facing Up to the Problem of Consciousness
core_readings.creator: David Chalmers
core_readings.note: Introduces the 'hard problem' distinction between explaining function/behavior and explaining experience — the conceptual spine of this whole trail.

further_reading.title: Could a Large Language Model Be Conscious?
further_reading.creator: David Chalmers
further_reading.note: Chalmers applies his own hard-problem framework directly to LLMs, weighing candidate indicators for and against — a direct sequel to the 1995 paper.

further_reading.title: Taking AI Welfare Seriously
further_reading.creator: Robert Long, Jeff Sebo, Patrick Butlin, et al.
further_reading.note: Moves from 'is it conscious' to the practical question of what moral precaution should look like under uncertainty — connects this trail to Futures Worth Wanting.

further_reading.title: On a Confusion About a Function of Consciousness
further_reading.creator: Ned Block
further_reading.note: Distinguishes 'access consciousness' (information available for reasoning/report) from 'phenomenal consciousness' (subjective experience) — useful for separating fluency from sentience.

further_reading.title: Consciousness Explained
further_reading.creator: Daniel Dennett
further_reading.note: A fully functionalist account that denies any residual hard problem — the strongest opposing position to Chalmers/Nagel, good for stress-testing your own position.

title: How Learning Systems Learn
question: What changes when a system is trained rather than explicitly programmed?
why_i_followed_this: The book emphasizes that modern AI systems are grown through optimization rather than handcrafted rule by rule. I wanted a trail that makes that distinction concrete without turning it into mysticism.
learning_objectives: Explain gradient descent, representations, embeddings, and loss functions.
Distinguish memorization, interpolation, generalization, and transfer.
Evaluate claims about emergence and phase changes in capability.
Identify what is and is not implied by the opacity of learned systems.
seminar_questions: In what sense are learned systems designed, and in what sense are they discovered?
Does opacity arise from scale, distributed representation, optimization, or all three?
When should an apparent capability jump count as emergence rather than measurement artifact?
How much does training data constrain the concepts a model can learn?

methods_lab.title: From Loss to Behavior
methods_lab.prompt: Trace a hypothetical model behavior backward through evaluation, inference, representation, training objective, and data.
methods_lab.deliverable: A causal diagram with annotated uncertainty at each layer.

position_memo.prompt: Does training a model make its behavior fundamentally less understandable than programming one?

my_current_position: Training changes the nature of control and explanation, but it does not make the resulting system unknowable. Explanations often need to be statistical, mechanistic, and system-level rather than source-code literal.
what_would_change_my_mind: If increasingly capable systems consistently resisted useful causal analysis despite major methodological advances, I would become more pessimistic about whether learned representations can be understood well enough for governance.
connected_trails: How Do We Know an AI System Is Safe?

core_readings.title: Deep Learning
core_readings.creator: Ian Goodfellow, Yoshua Bengio & Aaron Courville
core_readings.note: Still the standard technical reference for gradient descent, representation learning, and embeddings — the shared vocabulary layer under this whole concentration.

core_readings.title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
core_readings.creator: Jared Kaplan et al.
core_readings.note: Established that loss scales as a smooth power law in model size, data, and compute — the empirical basis for treating training as predictable rather than mystical.

core_readings.title: Are Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models a Mirage?
core_readings.creator: Rylan Schaeffer, Brando Miranda & Sanmi Koyejo
core_readings.note: Directly targets the emergence seminar question — shows several claimed capability 'jumps' disappear under continuous metrics, i.e. may be measurement artifacts rather than phase changes.

further_reading.title: Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models
further_reading.creator: Jason Wei et al.
further_reading.note: The paper that popularized the emergence framing that Schaeffer et al. later contest — read the two together.

further_reading.title: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models
further_reading.creator: Jordan Hoffmann et al. (Chinchilla)
further_reading.note: Revises the Kaplan-era scaling laws, showing most large models of that period were undertrained relative to their size — a good example of empirical self-correction in the field.

further_reading.title: Zoom In: An Introduction to Circuits
further_reading.creator: Chris Olah, Nick Cammarata, Ludwig Schubert, Gabriel Goh, Michael Petrov & Shan Carter
further_reading.note: Distill.pub essay arguing learned features and circuits can be identified and studied directly — bridges this trail into Looking Inside the Gray Box.

further_reading.title: Representation Learning: A Review and New Perspectives
further_reading.creator: Yoshua Bengio, Aaron Courville & Pascal Vincent
further_reading.note: Pre-LLM but still the clearest conceptual treatment of what a learned representation is and why depth/abstraction matter.

title: Alignment Is a Specification Problem
question: What does it mean to align a system when human values are plural, contextual, and difficult to specify?
why_i_followed_this: The word alignment often compresses several different problems: specifying objectives, learning preferences, preventing gaming, preserving corrigibility, and deciding whose values should govern the system.
learning_objectives: Distinguish outer alignment, inner alignment, reward misspecification, and value learning.
Explain how proxy objectives create Goodhart-style failures.
Evaluate RLHF and related methods as partial alignment techniques.
Analyze the political and ethical problem of deciding whose preferences count.
seminar_questions: Is alignment primarily an engineering problem, a political problem, or both?
Can preference aggregation produce legitimate values for a broadly deployed system?
When does adaptation to human feedback become sycophancy rather than alignment?
What should happen when individual preferences conflict with collective welfare?

methods_lab.title: Specification Under Value Conflict
methods_lab.prompt: Design an objective for an AI system with at least three stakeholders whose values conflict, then stress-test it for proxy failure and exclusion.
methods_lab.deliverable: A specification document with stakeholder map, failure modes, and revision.

position_memo.prompt: Can alignment be solved without a legitimate theory of whose values should be represented?

my_current_position: Alignment is partly technical and partly institutional. Better objectives and feedback methods matter, but no technical procedure removes the need to make contested decisions about values, power, and legitimacy.
what_would_change_my_mind: A method that reliably learns context-sensitive preferences, resists strategic manipulation, handles value conflict transparently, and earns broad acceptance would make me more optimistic.

core_readings.title: Concrete Problems in AI Safety
core_readings.creator: Dario Amodei, Chris Olah, Jacob Steinhardt, Paul Christiano, John Schulman & Dan Mané
core_readings.note: The field-defining taxonomy of specification and robustness failures (reward hacking, side effects, scalable oversight) — a clean entry point before the political dimension of alignment.

core_readings.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences
core_readings.creator: Paul Christiano et al.
core_readings.note: The technical origin of preference-based reward learning that RLHF later scaled up — read before InstructGPT / Constitutional AI.

core_readings.title: Categorizing Variants of Goodhart's Law
core_readings.creator: David Manheim & Scott Garrabrant
core_readings.note: Breaks 'when a measure becomes a target it ceases to be a good measure' into four distinct failure mechanisms — directly usable for the proxy-objective seminar question.

further_reading.title: Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback
further_reading.creator: Long Ouyang et al. (OpenAI, InstructGPT)
further_reading.note: The paper that made RLHF a standard production technique — a case study in partial alignment via feedback.

further_reading.title: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback
further_reading.creator: Yuntao Bai, Saurav Kadavath, Sandipan Kundu, et al. (Anthropic)
further_reading.note: Replaces some human feedback with model self-critique against written principles — a concrete attempt to make the 'whose values' question more transparent and auditable.

further_reading.title: Artificial Intelligence, Values, and Alignment
further_reading.creator: Iason Gabriel
further_reading.note: The clearest philosophical unpacking of what 'alignment' could mean: technical alignment, value alignment, and the political legitimacy question the position memo is really asking about.

further_reading.title: The Alignment Problem: Machine Learning and Human Values
further_reading.creator: Brian Christian
further_reading.note: INTRO AND CHAPTER 1 ONLYA - Accessible narrative synthesis connecting reward hacking, RLHF, and the specification problem — good for cross-checking your technical framing against a popular treatment.

title: Looking Inside the Gray Box
question: What kind of understanding is possible when a model's internal representations are distributed, dynamic, and large?
why_i_followed_this: I resisted the idea that modern models are simply black boxes. We can inspect activations, intervene on features, trace circuits, and test hypotheses. But partial visibility is not the same thing as complete explanation.
learning_objectives: Distinguish behavioral, mechanistic, probing, attribution, and causal explanations.
Evaluate strengths and weaknesses of major interpretability approaches.
Explain why correlational probes can mislead.
Assess whether interpretability results scale to safety-critical claims.
seminar_questions: What would count as understanding a neural network well enough for safety?
Can local circuit explanations support global claims about model behavior?
When is a probe discovering information rather than imposing an interpretation?
Should interpretability be treated as evidence, assurance, debugging, or all three?

methods_lab.title: Explanation Audit
methods_lab.prompt: Evaluate a hypothetical interpretability finding across faithfulness, causality, generality, and decision relevance.
methods_lab.deliverable: A structured interpretability audit with a go/no-go recommendation.

position_memo.prompt: What level of mechanistic understanding should be required before deploying a frontier model?

my_current_position: Gray box is more accurate than black box. We have meaningful access to model internals, but current methods remain partial and easy to overinterpret.
what_would_change_my_mind: Methods that reliably predict unseen behavior, identify hidden objectives, and support causal interventions across models would make me much more confident in interpretability as safety evidence.
connected_trails: How Do We Know an AI System Is Safe?

core_readings.title: Toy Models of Superposition
core_readings.creator: Nelson Elhage et al. (Anthropic)
core_readings.note: Explains why individual neurons are often polysemantic — models pack more features than they have dimensions — foundational for why interpretability is hard.

core_readings.title: Towards Monosemanticity: Decomposing Language Models with Dictionary Learning
core_readings.creator: Trenton Bricken et al. (Anthropic)
core_readings.note: Uses sparse autoencoders to extract more interpretable, single-meaning features from superposed activations — the direct technical follow-up to Toy Models of Superposition.

further_reading.title: Scaling Monosemanticity
further_reading.creator: Adly Templeton et al. (Anthropic)
further_reading.note: Extends dictionary-learning interpretability to a production-scale model and finds interpretable, causally-verifiable features — evidence relevant to the 'does this scale to safety-critical claims' seminar question.

further_reading.title: Towards a Rigorous Science of Interpretable Machine Learning
further_reading.creator: Finale Doshi-Velez & Been Kim
further_reading.note: Predates the circuits era but still the clearest framework for what would count as a validated interpretability claim — useful for the explanation-audit methods lab.

further_reading.title: Toward Transparent AI: A Survey on Interpreting the Inner Structures of Deep Neural Networks
further_reading.creator: Tilman Räuker, Anson Ho, Stephen Casper & Dylan Hadfield-Menell
further_reading.note: A broad map of the interpretability landscape (probing, attribution, causal intervention) — connective tissue across the methods being compared.

title: Human–AI Interaction
question: How do people decide when to trust, rely on, resist, or anthropomorphize an AI system?
why_i_followed_this: Many harms and misunderstandings emerge from how people interpret AI now. Fluency, confidence, and apparent social presence can shape trust long before the system is actually reliable.
learning_objectives: Distinguish trust, reliance, calibration, automation bias, and anthropomorphism.
Explain how interface design shapes perceived competence and agency.
Evaluate interventions intended to improve appropriate reliance.
Analyze human-AI systems as joint cognitive and organizational arrangements.
seminar_questions: Should trustworthy AI aim to increase trust or calibrate reliance?
How does conversational fluency distort judgments of competence?
When does anthropomorphism help users reason, and when does it mislead?
Where should responsibility lie when a human follows an AI recommendation?

methods_lab.title: Designing for Appropriate Reliance
methods_lab.prompt: Design a controlled study comparing a highly humanlike interface with a deliberately mechanical one.
methods_lab.deliverable: A preregistration-style study plan with hypotheses, measures, confounds, and analysis strategy.

position_memo.prompt: Should humanlike AI interfaces be treated as a safety feature, a usability feature, or a source of risk?

my_current_position: The goal should be calibrated reliance rather than generalized trust. Systems should make uncertainty, limits, and dependence on context easier to perceive.
what_would_change_my_mind: Evidence that humanlike interaction consistently improves error detection and decision quality in high-stakes settings would make me more comfortable with anthropomorphic design.

core_readings.title: Trust in Automation: Designing for Appropriate Reliance
core_readings.creator: John D. Lee & Katrina A. See
core_readings.note: The classic paper distinguishing trust from reliance and defining 'calibration' — very likely the direct source behind the 'calibrated reliance' framing in your current position.

core_readings.title: On Seeing Human: A Three-Factor Theory of Anthropomorphism
core_readings.creator: Nicholas Epley, Adam Waytz & John Cacioppo
core_readings.note: Explains anthropomorphism as driven by effectance motivation, sociality motivation, and elicited agent knowledge — a psychological model for when humanlike framing helps vs. misleads.

core_readings.title: Complacency and Bias in Human Use of Automation: An Attentional Integration
core_readings.creator: Raja Parasuraman & Dietrich Manzey
core_readings.note: Synthesizes the automation-bias literature and the attentional mechanisms behind over-reliance — directly relevant to the calibrated-reliance position.

further_reading.title: Does the Whole Exceed Its Parts? The Effect of AI Explanations on Complementary Team Performance
further_reading.creator: Gagan Bansal et al.
further_reading.note: Empirically tests whether explanations actually improve human+AI joint decisions — a sobering result set for the 'explainability as safety feature' question.

further_reading.title: To Trust or to Think: Cognitive Forcing Functions Can Reduce Overreliance on AI
further_reading.creator: Zana Buçinca, Maja Barbara Malaya & Krzysztof Z. Gajos
further_reading.note: Tests interface interventions designed to slow people down before accepting an AI recommendation — good raw material for the reliance-study methods lab.

further_reading.title: Effect of Confidence and Explanation on Accuracy and Trust Calibration in AI-Assisted Decision Making
further_reading.creator: Yunfeng Zhang, Q. Vera Liao & Rachel K. E. Bellamy
further_reading.note: Shows confidence scores and explanations don't straightforwardly improve calibration and can even backfire — pairs well with the Bansal et al. paper.

title: AI as a Sociotechnical System
question: What changes when the unit of analysis is not the model, but the people, institutions, infrastructure, and incentives around it?
why_i_followed_this: I kept wanting to widen the frame to include organizations, deployment choices, data pipelines, labor, power, regulation, and the ways responsibility gets distributed across a system.
learning_objectives: Define sociotechnical systems and identify their interacting layers.
Analyze how organizational incentives shape deployment risk.
Explain diffusion of responsibility, moral crumple zones, and normalization of deviance.
Compare technical fixes with institutional and workflow interventions.
seminar_questions: Where should the boundary of an AI system be drawn for accountability?
How do organizations convert uncertain model outputs into real-world decisions?
When does a human reviewer provide oversight rather than liability transfer?
Which failures are best addressed through model changes, workflow design, or governance?

methods_lab.title: Mapping the Whole System
methods_lab.prompt: Choose an AI-enabled workflow and map actors, incentives, data flows, decision points, escalation paths, and failure modes.
methods_lab.deliverable: A sociotechnical system map plus a one-page intervention brief.

position_memo.prompt: Why do AI safety debates over-focus on models and under-focus on organizations?

my_current_position: Model behavior cannot be separated cleanly from the systems that deploy, interpret, and reward it. Many failures emerge from the interaction between technical and institutional layers.
what_would_change_my_mind: If model-intrinsic interventions repeatedly eliminated major deployment failures across very different organizations, I would place more weight on the model as the primary unit of analysis.
connected_trails: How Do We Know an AI System Is Safe?

core_readings.title: Fairness and Abstraction in Sociotechnical Systems
core_readings.creator: Andrew D. Selbst, danah boyd, Sorelle A. Friedler, Suresh Venkatasubramanian & Janet Vertesi
core_readings.note: Names the 'abstraction traps' that happen when a sociotechnical problem gets treated as a purely technical one — a strong scaffold for the sociotechnical-system-map methods lab.

core_readings.title: The Challenger Launch Decision
core_readings.creator: Diane Vaughan
core_readings.note: The canonical case study in normalization of deviance — how an organization incrementally redefines acceptable risk until disaster looks routine.

core_readings.title: Moral Crumple Zones: Cautionary Tales in Human-Robot Interaction
core_readings.creator: Madeleine Clare Elish
core_readings.note: Coins the term for how humans absorb blame for failures that are really systemic — directly answers the seminar question about where accountability actually lands.

further_reading.title: Datasheets for Datasets
further_reading.creator: Timnit Gebru et al.
further_reading.note: A concrete documentation-and-accountability intervention at the data layer — a good example of a workflow-level, not model-level, fix.

further_reading.title: Seeing Without Knowing: Limitations of the Transparency Ideal and Its Application to Algorithmic Accountability
further_reading.creator: Mike Ananny & Kate Crawford
further_reading.note: Argues transparency alone doesn't produce accountability in complex sociotechnical systems — a useful counterpoint if the intervention brief leans on disclosure.

further_reading.title: Machine Behaviour
further_reading.creator: Iyad Rahwan et al.
further_reading.note: Nature paper proposing AI systems in the wild need to be studied empirically as a behavioral science, not just engineered as artifacts — widens the unit of analysis the way this trail's question asks.

title: How Do We Know an AI System Is Safe?
question: What evidence should count when we claim that an AI system is safe enough to deploy?
why_i_followed_this: The book makes strong claims about future danger, while current safety practices rely on benchmarks, red teaming, evaluations, and staged deployment. I wanted to examine what these methods can actually establish.
learning_objectives: Distinguish capability evaluation, safety evaluation, assurance, monitoring, and incident analysis.
Evaluate benchmark validity, ecological validity, and distribution shift.
Explain strengths and limitations of red teaming and model organisms.
Design evidence portfolios rather than relying on single metrics.
seminar_questions: Can a finite evaluation support a claim about open-ended deployment?
How should evaluation account for strategic adaptation or evaluator awareness?
What is the relationship between pre-deployment evaluation and post-deployment monitoring?
Who should decide what counts as sufficient evidence?

methods_lab.title: Build a Safety Case
methods_lab.prompt: Create a structured safety case for a hypothetical high-capability model.
methods_lab.deliverable: A safety-case diagram and a two-page written justification.

position_memo.prompt: What evidence should be required before a frontier model is released beyond controlled testing?

my_current_position: No single benchmark or red-team exercise can establish safety. The strongest approach is a layered evidence portfolio combining behavior, mechanisms, security, staged deployment, and monitoring.
what_would_change_my_mind: A validated evaluation suite that consistently predicts serious deployment failures across models and contexts would make me more willing to treat pre-deployment testing as a stronger guarantee.

core_readings.title: Model Evaluation for Extreme Risks
core_readings.creator: Toby Shevlane, Sebastian Farquhar, Ben Garfinkel, et al.
core_readings.note: The framework paper distinguishing dangerous-capability evaluations from alignment (propensity) evaluations — almost certainly the direct source of this trail's framing.

core_readings.title: Safety Cases: How to Justify the Safety of Advanced AI Systems
core_readings.creator: Joshua Clymer, Nick Gabrieli, David Krueger & Thomas Larsen
core_readings.note: Adapts the 'safety case' methodology from aviation/nuclear engineering to frontier AI — a structured argument-plus-evidence approach rather than a single benchmark score.

core_readings.title: Red Teaming Language Models with Language Models
core_readings.creator: Ethan Perez et al.
core_readings.note: Shows red-teaming itself can be partly automated and scaled — useful for weighing what red-teaming can and can't establish.

further_reading.title: Unsolved Problems in ML Safety
further_reading.creator: Dan Hendrycks, Nicholas Carlini, John Schulman & Jacob Steinhardt
further_reading.note: A broad taxonomy (robustness, monitoring, alignment, systemic safety) that complements Shevlane et al.'s narrower extreme-risk focus.

further_reading.title: Frontier Safety Framework and Responsible Scaling Policy (public policy documents)
further_reading.creator: Google DeepMind and Anthropic
further_reading.note: The actual industry implementations of staged-deployment evaluation gates — worth reading as primary sources rather than academic proposals, and useful for the safety-case methods lab.

further_reading.title: International AI Safety Report
further_reading.creator: Yoshua Bengio et al. (chair), international expert panel
further_reading.note: A government-commissioned synthesis of the evaluation and evidence landscape across many labs and countries — relevant to 'who should decide what counts as sufficient evidence.'

title: Reasoning About Catastrophic Risk
question: How should we reason when probabilities are deeply uncertain but some outcomes may be irreversible?
why_i_followed_this: The book repeatedly asks the reader to act under extreme uncertainty. I wanted to understand when precaution is rational, when it becomes unfalsifiable, and how to distinguish serious tail-risk analysis from rhetoric that cannot be updated.
learning_objectives: Distinguish risk, uncertainty, ambiguity, and Knightian uncertainty.
Explain expected value, tail risk, fat tails, and risk asymmetry.
Evaluate precautionary and robust decision-making frameworks.
Identify failure modes such as scope insensitivity and Pascal's Mugging.
seminar_questions: How should policy respond when catastrophic probabilities cannot be calibrated?
When does the scale of harm justify action despite weak probability estimates?
What makes a precautionary argument updateable rather than self-sealing?
How should reversible and irreversible decisions be treated differently?

methods_lab.title: Decision-Making Under Deep Uncertainty
methods_lab.prompt: Compare expected value, maximin, minimax regret, and robust decision-making for the same hypothetical AI policy choice.
methods_lab.deliverable: A decision matrix with sensitivity analysis and a final recommendation.

position_memo.prompt: When is uncertainty a reason to slow down, and when is it an excuse to avoid deciding?

my_current_position: Uncertainty should sometimes strengthen the case for precaution, especially when harms are irreversible. But the argument must still specify mechanisms, thresholds, and what evidence would justify changing course.
what_would_change_my_mind: Reliable calibration methods for unprecedented technological risks would substantially change how I weight precaution.
connected_trails: How Do We Know an AI System Is Safe?

core_readings.title: Existential Risk Prevention as Global Priority
core_readings.creator: Nick Bostrom
core_readings.note: Lays out the formal case for why even tiny probabilities of existential harm can dominate expected-value calculations — the paper most directly under scrutiny in this trail's seminar questions.

core_readings.title: The Precipice: Existential Risk and the Future of Humanity
core_readings.creator: Toby Ord
core_readings.note: REVIEW ONLY - The most complete book-length treatment of existential risk reasoning, including explicit probability estimates and their epistemic limits.

core_readings.title: On Modeling and Interpreting the Economics of Catastrophic Climate Change
core_readings.creator: Martin Weitzman
core_readings.note: Origin of the 'dismal theorem': under fat-tailed uncertainty about catastrophic outcomes, standard expected-value cost-benefit analysis can break down. Useful for comparing precautionary vs. expected-value framings.

further_reading.title: The Vulnerable World Hypothesis
further_reading.creator: Nick Bostrom
further_reading.note: Asks what governance would be needed if technological progress could, by default, produce a civilization-ending capability — a bridge into Governing Frontier AI.

further_reading.title: Pascal's Mugging
further_reading.creator: Nick Bostrom
further_reading.note: Short treatment of the failure mode where tiny probabilities of vast outcomes can be used to demand almost anything — useful for testing when precaution becomes unfalsifiable.

further_reading.title: Radical Uncertainty: Decision-Making Beyond the Numbers
further_reading.creator: John Kay & Mervyn King
further_reading.note: A sustained argument for treating deep (Knightian) uncertainty differently from calculable risk — ballast for the reversible-vs-irreversible seminar question.

title: Governing Frontier AI
question: What forms of governance could constrain frontier AI without creating an equally dangerous concentration of power?
why_i_followed_this: The later chapters move from diagnosis to global prohibition, compute monitoring, international enforcement, and even military action. I wanted to examine the space between voluntary commitments and an emergency regime with extraordinary coercive power.
learning_objectives: Compare licensing, audits, compute governance, liability, monitoring, and international coordination.
Analyze race dynamics, verification problems, and regulatory arbitrage.
Evaluate legitimacy, accountability, and concentration-of-power risks.
Distinguish symbolic regulation from institution-building.
seminar_questions: Which governance mechanisms remain effective as algorithms become more efficient?
How can verification work when major actors have incentives to conceal capabilities?
What safeguards are needed when a safety regime creates concentrated power?
How should governance distinguish frontier research, deployment, publication, and safety work?

methods_lab.title: Design a Layered Governance Regime
methods_lab.prompt: Design a framework using firm-level controls, national regulation, infrastructure governance, and international coordination.
methods_lab.deliverable: A governance architecture diagram plus a risk register for the regime itself.

position_memo.prompt: What is the strongest legitimate governance regime that could plausibly constrain frontier AI?

my_current_position: Voluntary commitments are insufficient and domestic regulation alone is incomplete. I support layered governance, but remain skeptical of indefinite emergency powers, pervasive surveillance, and preventive violence.
what_would_change_my_mind: Evidence that narrower governance repeatedly failed while verified compute controls reliably reduced danger without major abuse would move me toward stronger centralized mechanisms.

core_readings.title: Frontier AI Regulation: Managing Emerging Risks to Public Safety
core_readings.creator: Markus Anderljung, Joslyn Barnhart, Anton Korinek, Jade Leung, et al.
core_readings.note: The most widely cited proposal for licensing, registration, and reporting requirements specifically for frontier-scale models — a baseline governance architecture to stress-test.

core_readings.title: International Institutions for Advanced AI
core_readings.creator: Lewis Ho, Joslyn Barnhart, Robert Trager, Yoshua Bengio, et al.
core_readings.note: Surveys existing international-institution models (from IAEA to ICAO) as templates for AI-specific coordination — directly usable for the governance-architecture diagram.

core_readings.title: Computing Power and the Governance of Artificial Intelligence
core_readings.creator: Girish Sastry et al.
core_readings.note: Makes the case for compute as the most measurable and governable input to frontier AI development — the technical backbone for any compute-governance proposal.

further_reading.title: Towards Best Practices in AGI Safety and Governance
further_reading.creator: Jonas Schuett, Noemi Dreksler, Markus Anderljung, David McCaffary, Lennart Heim, Emma Bluemke & Ben Garfinkel
further_reading.note: A GovAI survey of practitioner views on what 'best practice' governance currently looks like inside labs — grounding for firm-level controls in the layered-regime design.

further_reading.title: Thinking About Risks From AI: Accidents, Misuse and Structure
further_reading.creator: Remco Zwetsloot & Allan Dafoe
further_reading.note: Distinguishes accident, misuse, and structural risk as separate categories requiring different governance tools — a clean framework for sorting which mechanism addresses which failure mode.

title: Futures Worth Wanting
question: What kind of future are we trying to preserve, and who gets to decide?
why_i_followed_this: The book is clear about the future it wants to prevent, but less developed on the futures worth building. Questions about abundance, enhancement, artificial minds, value lock-in, legitimacy, and plural visions of flourishing kept opening beyond the extinction argument.
learning_objectives: Compare competing visions of technological flourishing.
Explain value lock-in, longtermism, intergenerational justice, and moral uncertainty.
Analyze the status of artificial persons and enhanced humans.
Evaluate how democratic processes can govern high-stakes long-term choices.
seminar_questions: Is survival enough, or does safety need a positive theory of flourishing?
How should future artificial minds be represented in political decisions?
Can value lock-in ever be justified?
What decisions may present generations make on behalf of distant futures?

methods_lab.title: Plural Futures Workshop
methods_lab.prompt: Develop three contrasting but coherent visions of a good AI-enabled future and identify their beneficiaries, exclusions, governance needs, and failure modes.
methods_lab.deliverable: A comparative futures matrix and reflection on irreducible value conflict.

position_memo.prompt: What obligations do present institutions have to future humans, enhanced humans, and possible artificial minds?

my_current_position: Safety cannot be separated completely from what is being protected. A future that avoids extinction but freezes injustice, excludes new moral patients, or removes democratic agency may still be a profound failure.
what_would_change_my_mind: A compelling argument that long-term coordination requires temporary value stabilization, combined with strong democratic safeguards and reversibility, would make me less resistant to limited forms of value preservation.

core_readings.title: What We Owe the Future
core_readings.creator: William MacAskill
core_readings.note: The most accessible full case for longtermism and intergenerational obligation — directly engages the seminar question about what present institutions owe future/possible minds.

core_readings.title: Reasons and Persons
core_readings.creator: Derek Parfit
core_readings.note: The philosophical bedrock for population ethics and obligations to future people — dense but foundational underneath MacAskill's popularization.

core_readings.title: Deep Utopia: Life and Meaning in a Solved World
core_readings.creator: Nick Bostrom
core_readings.note: Unlike Bostrom's earlier extinction-focused work, this asks what a successful outcome would actually look like and whether it's still meaningful — a direct answer to 'is survival enough.'

further_reading.title: The Case for Strong Longtermism
further_reading.creator: Hilary Greaves & William MacAskill
further_reading.note: The more technical, philosophy-journal version of the longtermism argument — useful if the position memo should engage the strongest academic form rather than the popular one.

further_reading.title: Reality+: Virtual Worlds and the Problems of Philosophy
further_reading.creator: David Chalmers
further_reading.note: Extends questions about moral status and meaning to simulated/digital minds — relevant if the futures matrix includes artificial persons as stakeholders.

further_reading.title: Taking AI Welfare Seriously
further_reading.creator: Robert Long, Jeff Sebo, Patrick Butlin, et al.
further_reading.note: Cross-listed from Minds Made of Machines — the practical companion to the philosophical arguments here about what present institutions owe possible future moral patients.
